faqs.a: The 2017 and 2018 model years get the most attention because they were the first with the 1.5L turbo engine and had the highest rate of reported fuel dilution complaints, before Honda rolled out software and calibration updates to address it.

faqs.q: Which Honda CR-V has the least problems?
faqs.a: Fourth-generation models from 2013, 2014, and 2016 are commonly cited as safe choices since they use a naturally aspirated 2.4L engine with no turbo-related fuel dilution risk. Later fifth-generation years, 2020 to 2022, are also solid once the early turbo fixes were in place.

faqs.q: How much does it cost to fix CR-V fuel dilution?
faqs.a: If caught early and addressed with Honda's software or PCM update, the fix can be free under warranty or a covered service campaign. If the dilution went unaddressed long enough to damage engine bearings or internal components, repair costs can run into the thousands of dollars.

title: Toyota RAV4 Common Problems and the Best Years to Buy (2026 Guide)
metaTitle: Toyota RAV4 Common Problems & Best Years (2026)
metaDescription: See which Toyota RAV4 years have the most reported problems and which are the safest to buy used, plus how to check any RAV4's history before you buy in 2026.
bodyHtml: <p>The Toyota RAV4 has a strong reputation as one of the most dependable compact SUVs on the road, and that reputation is largely earned. But reliable does not mean problem-free, and certain RAV4 model years have documented issues that are worth knowing about before you buy one used. Some of these are minor annoyances fixed with a software update. Others are expensive enough to change which specific year and trim you should target.</p><p>Below is a factual breakdown of the RAV4's most commonly reported problems, which years to lean toward, which years deserve extra scrutiny, and how to check the specific history of any RAV4 you are considering before you hand over a deposit.</p><h2>Common Toyota RAV4 Problems by Year</h2><p>These are the issues most frequently reported by RAV4 owners and independent mechanics. Not every vehicle in these model years will experience these problems, but they show up often enough to be worth checking for during a test drive or pre-purchase inspection.</p><table><thead><tr><th>Problem</th><th>Affected Years / Generation</th><th>Symptoms</th><th>Rough Repair Cost</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Harsh or hesitant low-speed shifting</td><td>2019-2021 (fifth generation, 8-speed automatic)</td><td>Jerky or delayed shifts between 1st and 3rd gear, most noticeable when cold or accelerating from a stop</td><td>$0 for a dealer software reflash; $3,500-$4,500 in rare cases needing transmission work</td></tr><tr><td>Fuel tank will not fill to full</td><td>2019-2020 RAV4 Hybrid and some AWD gas models</td><td>Fuel pump clicks off well before the tank is full, reducing real-world range</td><td>Free if an open dealer campaign still applies; otherwise no reliable aftermarket fix</td></tr><tr><td>Excessive oil consumption</td><td>Some 2006-2008 four-cylinder models (2AZ-FE engine)</td><td>Oil warning light, burning oil smell, needing to top off oil between changes</td><td>$0 to monitor and top off; $2,500-$4,000 if a repair or rebuild becomes necessary</td></tr><tr><td>Infotainment freezing or lag</td><td>2019-2022</td><td>Touchscreen delays, backup camera taking a moment to load, occasional Bluetooth dropouts</td><td>$0 software update up to $600-$900 for a head unit replacement</td></tr><tr><td>AWD driveline shudder</td><td>Scattered reports on fourth and fifth generation AWD models</td><td>Vibration or shudder felt during slow, tight turns</td><td>$150 for a fluid service up to $800-$1,200 for a coupling replacement</td></tr><tr><td>12V battery drain</td><td>2019-2022, reported more often on Hybrid models</td><td>Car will not start after sitting several days, dashboard warning lights</td><td>$150-$250 for a battery; more if a parasitic draw needs to be traced</td></tr></tbody></table><p>None of these issues are exclusive to Toyota. Every mass-market SUV has a list like this one. What matters is knowing which years cluster which problems so you can shop with your eyes open.</p><h2>Most Reliable RAV4 Years to Buy</h2><p>If you want to minimize the chances of dealing with any of the issues above, a few model years stand out as safer bets among used RAV4 buyers and independent mechanics.</p><ul><li><strong>2016-2018 (fourth generation):</strong> These years come after Toyota worked out most of the early fourth-generation kinks and before the 8-speed transmission complaints that showed up in the redesigned fifth generation. They are widely considered some of the most trouble-free RAV4 years on the used market.</li><li><strong>2010-2012 (third generation):</strong> By this point Toyota had addressed the oil consumption concerns seen in some earlier four-cylinder engines, and these years benefit from a simpler, well-proven drivetrain.</li><li><strong>2022-2024 (fifth generation, post-update):</strong> Toyota issued multiple software updates and running changes to address the harsh-shifting complaints from 2019-2021. Later fifth-generation models, especially those with a documented reflash in their service history, report far fewer transmission complaints.</li></ul><p>Across the board, the RAV4 Hybrid has a loyal following for its fuel economy and generally strong long-term reliability, though it is worth checking specifically for the fuel tank fill issue and 12V battery drain on 2019-2020 Hybrid models.</p><h2>RAV4 Years to Be Cautious About</h2><p>A few model years deserve a closer look before you commit, not because they are bad vehicles overall, but because they carry a higher concentration of the known issues above.</p><ul><li><strong>2019-2020:</strong> The first two years of the fifth generation carry the bulk of the transmission hesitation and fuel tank fill complaints. Many of these were addressed under dealer service campaigns, so confirming whether a specific vehicle already received the fix is worthwhile.</li><li><strong>2006-2008:</strong> If you are looking at an older, budget-friendly RAV4, ask for oil change records and check the oil level and color yourself. A well-maintained example from these years can still be a fine daily driver, but consistent oil top-offs are a red flag worth walking away from.</li></ul><p>In both cases, the fix is not to avoid the year entirely, it is to ask for maintenance records, get a pre-purchase inspection, and confirm any recall or service campaign work was actually completed.</p><h2>Is a Used RAV4 Worth Buying?</h2><p>For most shoppers, yes. The RAV4 has consistently been one of the best-selling and most trusted compact SUVs in the United States for good reason: strong crash test performance, solid resale value, low running costs, and a hybrid option that genuinely delivers real-world fuel savings. Parts are widely available, most mechanics know the platform well, and the RAV4's conventional engineering, aside from the issues noted above, tends to age gracefully.</p><p>That said, worth buying always depends on the individual vehicle in front of you, not just the model name. Two RAV4s from the same year can have very different histories. Before you buy any used RAV4, it is worth confirming:</p><ul><li>Full maintenance history, especially oil changes and transmission service</li><li>Whether any relevant recalls or service campaigns were completed</li><li>A pre-purchase inspection by an independent mechanic, not just the seller's word</li><li>The vehicle's title and accident history</li></ul><h2>How to Check a Specific RAV4 Before You Buy</h2><p>Knowing which model years to favor is only half the picture. The other half is confirming that the specific RAV4 you are looking at has not been in a serious accident, does not carry a salvage or rebuilt title, and has an odometer reading that actually matches its history.</p><p>Before you buy, pull the vehicle's VIN and check it for:</p><ul><li><strong>Title brands:</strong> salvage, rebuilt, flood, or lemon titles that a seller may not disclose upfront</li><li><strong>Accident and damage history:</strong> prior reported collisions, airbag deployments, or structural damage</li><li><strong>Odometer readings:</strong> inconsistencies that could point to a rollback</li><li><strong>Open recalls:</strong> including any outstanding transmission software or fuel tank campaigns tied to that specific VIN</li></ul><p>A CarWorthIt VIN report pulls this information together in one place, so you can see a RAV4's real history before you negotiate a price or hand over a deposit. For a vehicle you might keep for the next decade, spending a few minutes checking its background is one of the cheapest forms of insurance you can buy.</p>

faqs.q: What is the most common problem with the Toyota RAV4?
faqs.a: The most frequently reported issue on used RAV4s is harsh or hesitant low-speed shifting on 2019-2021 models with the 8-speed automatic transmission. Many cases were resolved with a dealer software update, so it is worth asking whether a specific vehicle already received it.

faqs.q: Which RAV4 years should I avoid?
faqs.a: No RAV4 year needs to be avoided outright, but 2019-2020 models carry the highest concentration of transmission and fuel tank fill complaints, and some 2006-2008 four-cylinder models have a history of excessive oil consumption. Maintenance records matter more than the model year alone.

faqs.q: Is the RAV4 Hybrid reliable?
faqs.a: Generally yes. The RAV4 Hybrid has a strong reliability reputation and delivers real-world fuel savings, though 2019-2020 Hybrid models are worth checking specifically for the fuel tank fill issue and for signs of 12V battery drain.

faqs.q: How many miles can a Toyota RAV4 last?
faqs.a: With regular maintenance, it is common for RAV4s to reach 200,000 miles or more. Staying on top of the oil change schedule and addressing any transmission or driveline symptoms early goes a long way toward reaching that mileage.

title: Chevy Silverado 1500 Common Problems in 2026 (and the Best Years to Buy)
metaTitle: Chevy Silverado 1500 Problems & Best Years (2026)
metaDescription: Thinking about buying a used Chevy Silverado 1500? Learn the most common engine and transmission problems, the best years to buy, and how to check a VIN first.
bodyHtml: <p>The Chevy Silverado 1500 is one of the best-selling trucks in America, and it is easy to see why. It is tough, capable, and available with a wide range of engines to match nearly any budget or towing need. But like any used vehicle, some Silverado model years and engine combinations have earned a reputation for specific mechanical issues. Knowing which problems to watch for, and which years tend to hold up best, can save you thousands of dollars and a lot of frustration down the road.</p><h2>Common Chevy Silverado 1500 Problems</h2><p>Most of the issues owners report with the Silverado 1500 trace back to a handful of engines and transmissions used across several model years. Here is a quick breakdown of the problems that come up most often in owner forums, independent shop repair data, and used truck inspections.</p><table><thead><tr><th>Problem</th><th>Affected Years or Engine</th><th>Symptoms</th><th>Rough Repair Cost</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>AFM excessive oil consumption</td><td>5.3L V8, 2007-2013</td><td>Burns a quart of oil every 1,000-2,000 miles, blue smoke on cold start</td><td>$1,500-$4,000 for an AFM disabler or related engine work</td></tr><tr><td>Lifter tick and lifter failure</td><td>5.3L and 6.2L V8 with AFM or DFM, some 2014-2018 and 2019 and newer models</td><td>Persistent ticking or knocking from the engine, check engine light, rough idle</td><td>$1,800-$3,500 for lifter and camshaft replacement</td></tr><tr><td>8-speed automatic transmission shudder</td><td>2015-2019 models with the 8L90 transmission</td><td>Shudder or hesitation between 30 and 45 mph, hard or delayed shifts</td><td>$150-$300 for a fluid and software update, $3,000 or more if internal repair is needed</td></tr><tr><td>Brake booster or vacuum pump failure</td><td>2014-2019 models, more common on diesel trucks and some gas V8s</td><td>Hard or high brake pedal, longer stopping distance, dash warning light</td><td>$400-$900</td></tr><tr><td>Transmission cooler line leaks</td><td>Various years, more common on higher-mileage trucks</td><td>Fluid spots under the truck, low or dark transmission fluid</td><td>$150-$400</td></tr><tr><td>Dashboard and interior trim cracking</td><td>2007-2013 (GMT900 body style)</td><td>Cracked or sun-damaged dash pad, faded or peeling interior trim</td><td>$300-$800 for a dash cover or replacement</td></tr></tbody></table><h2>Most Reliable Silverado Years and Engines to Buy</h2><p>If you want to stack the odds in your favor, focus on the model years and engines that avoid the worst of the known issues above.</p><ul><li><strong>2021-2024 Silverado 1500:</strong> the current generation benefits from years of software refinement to the AFM and DFM cylinder deactivation systems and a more mature 8-speed or 10-speed transmission.</li><li><strong>2016-2018 Silverado 1500 with the 5.3L V8:</strong> these trucks largely sidestep both the heavy oil-burning complaints tied to the earlier AFM system and the early bugs in the redesigned 8-speed automatic.</li><li><strong>Trucks with the 6.2L V8 and 10-speed transmission:</strong> this drivetrain combination, shared with other GM truck applications, generally has a strong reliability track record.</li><li><strong>Duramax diesel-equipped Silverados (2020 and newer):</strong> a good option for high-mileage highway driving and towing, though diesel trucks carry their own maintenance costs and should be inspected by a diesel specialist.</li></ul><h2>Silverado Years and Engines to Be Cautious About</h2><p>A few specific combinations show up again and again in complaint databases and repair shop notes. None of them make the Silverado a bad truck, but they are worth budgeting for or negotiating on price if you are looking at one of these trucks.</p><h3>5.3L AFM Oil Consumption (2007-2013)</h3><p>Active Fuel Management, GM's cylinder deactivation system, was introduced to improve fuel economy by shutting down four cylinders during light-load driving. On many 2007-2013 trucks it led to excessive oil consumption, sometimes a full quart every 1,000 to 2,000 miles. Left unchecked, low oil levels can accelerate wear on rings, valve guides, and lifters. Ask for oil change and top-off records, and check the dipstick yourself before buying.</p><h3>Lifter Tick on AFM and DFM Engines (2014 and Newer)</h3><p>The next-generation Dynamic Fuel Management system, used on 2019 and newer 5.3L and 6.2L engines, and the earlier AFM system on 2014-2018 trucks, have both been linked to lifter failure and a noticeable ticking noise from the valvetrain. A cold start tick that fades after a minute is common and usually harmless, but a persistent tick under load or a check engine light for a misfire is worth having diagnosed before you buy.</p><h3>8-Speed Transmission Shudder (2015-2019)</h3><p>The 8L90 8-speed automatic, introduced for the 2015 model year, has been associated with shudder, hesitation, and rough shifting in a portion of trucks, most often felt in the 30 to 45 mph range during light acceleration. GM issued software updates and revised fluid to address it, so ask whether the truck has received the relevant transmission control module update.</p><h2>Is a Used Chevy Silverado 1500 Worth Buying?</h2><p>Yes, for most buyers. The Silverado 1500 remains one of the most capable and comfortable full-size trucks on the used market, and the vast majority of the issues above are known, well-documented, and fixable rather than mysterious or catastrophic. The key is matching your budget to the right engine and model year, confirming maintenance history, and getting a pre-purchase inspection that specifically checks oil consumption, transmission behavior, and lifter noise. A Silverado that has been well maintained, especially one with documented oil changes and no history of hard commercial use, can easily deliver 200,000 miles or more.</p><h2>How to Check a Specific Silverado Before You Buy</h2><p>Every individual truck has its own history, and a clean-looking Silverado can still be hiding expensive problems. Before you hand over any money, it is worth pulling a full vehicle history report using the VIN on the dashboard or door jamb. A VIN check can help confirm:</p><ul><li>Whether the title is clean or branded as salvage, flood, rebuilt, or lemon</li><li>Prior accident or damage history, including frame and structural damage</li><li>Whether the odometer reading matches the vehicle's reported history, to help rule out rollback</li><li>Any open safety recalls that have not yet been completed</li><li>Signs of prior fleet, commercial, towing, or snowplow use, which can mean heavier wear on the frame, suspension, and transmission</li></ul><p>Running the VIN through CarWorthIt before you buy takes just a couple of minutes and can flag red flags that are not visible on a test drive or a quick walk-around. Combined with a mechanic's inspection focused on the AFM, lifter, and transmission issues covered above, it gives you a much clearer picture of what you are actually buying before you sign anything.</p>

faqs.q: What is the most common problem with the Chevy Silverado 1500?
faqs.a: The most widely reported issue is excessive oil consumption tied to the Active Fuel Management system on 5.3L V8 engines built between 2007 and 2013. Lifter tick on later AFM and Dynamic Fuel Management engines is also a common complaint.

faqs.q: Which Silverado years should I avoid?
faqs.a: There is no single year to avoid outright, but 2007-2013 trucks with the 5.3L V8 have the highest rate of oil consumption complaints, and early 8-speed automatic trucks from 2015 and 2016 saw the most transmission shudder issues before GM issued fixes. A pre-purchase inspection matters more than the model year alone.

faqs.q: Is the 5.3L V8 reliable in the Silverado?
faqs.a: Overall, yes. The 5.3L V8 is one of the most widely used truck engines in the country and is generally durable when properly maintained. The main weak points are AFM-related oil consumption on 2007-2013 models and occasional lifter tick on later versions, both of which are known issues that a mechanic can check for before purchase.

faqs.q: How can I tell if a used Silverado was used for towing or plowing?
faqs.a: Look for a plow prep package, extra wiring or mounting brackets on the front bumper, aftermarket transmission coolers, worn or replaced front suspension components, and uneven tire wear. A vehicle history report can also sometimes show prior commercial or fleet registration, which is a useful clue alongside a physical inspection.

title: Ford Explorer Common Problems (and the Years to Avoid) in 2026
metaTitle: Ford Explorer Common Problems & Years to Avoid (2026)
metaDescription: Considering a used Ford Explorer? Learn the most common problems by generation, which years to avoid, and how to check any Explorer's history before you buy.
bodyHtml: <p>The Ford Explorer has been one of America's best selling three row SUVs for decades, and it remains a common choice for families cross shopping midsize SUVs on the used market. But not every model year has aged the same way. Certain generations carry well documented issues ranging from exhaust odor entering the cabin to expensive internal water pump failures and all wheel drive component problems. None of this means every Explorer from those years is a bad buy, but it does mean a shopper needs to know what to check before handing over money. Below is a rundown of the Explorer's most common problem areas by generation, which years tend to be safer bets, which years deserve extra caution, and how to verify the history of any specific Explorer you are considering.</p><h2>Common Ford Explorer Problems by Generation</h2><p>Ford has built the Explorer across six generations since 1991, and while the overall design has held up reasonably well against rivals like the Toyota Highlander and Honda Pilot, a handful of recurring issues show up often enough in owner complaints, technical service bulletins, and repair shop data to be worth flagging before you buy. The fourth generation ran from 2006 to 2010, the fifth generation from 2011 to 2019, and the current sixth generation began in 2020 with a switch to a rear wheel drive based platform shared with the Lincoln Aviator.</p><table><thead><tr><th>Problem</th><th>Affected Years or Generation</th><th>Symptoms</th><th>Rough Repair Cost</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Exhaust odor, possible carbon monoxide entering cabin</td><td>2011-2017 (fifth generation)</td><td>Exhaust smell inside the cabin, especially at idle, low speed, or during acceleration</td><td>$200-$1,500 depending on cause (seals, exhaust manifold, liftgate seal)</td></tr><tr><td>Internal water pump failure (3.5L V6)</td><td>2011-2019 models with the 3.5L engine</td><td>Coolant mixing with oil, milky residue on the dipstick, overheating, potential engine damage if ignored</td><td>$1,200-$2,000 (labor intensive since the pump sits behind the timing cover)</td></tr><tr><td>Power Transfer Unit (PTU) failure</td><td>AWD models, especially 2011-2015</td><td>Whining or grinding noise near the front differential, fluid leaks, vibration under load</td><td>$1,000-$2,200 to replace</td></tr><tr><td>Transmission hesitation or rough shifting</td><td>2020-2021 (early sixth generation, rear drive platform)</td><td>Hesitation on acceleration, delayed or rough shifts, occasional stalling reports</td><td>$150-$4,000+ (software update up to full transmission replacement)</td></tr><tr><td>Door latch and window regulator faults</td><td>2011-2017</td><td>Doors that will not latch or lock properly, windows that are slow or fail to move</td><td>$150-$450 per door</td></tr><tr><td>Infotainment and electrical glitches</td><td>2013-2019</td><td>Backup camera dropouts, touchscreen freezing or rebooting, unexplained battery drain</td><td>$100-$600</td></tr></tbody></table><h2>Most Reliable Ford Explorer Years to Buy</h2><p>If reliability is the top priority, the later years of each generation tend to be the safer bet, since Ford typically works out early production issues through running changes and technical service bulletins. The 2018 and 2019 Explorers arrived after most of the fifth generation exhaust and door latch fixes had been rolled into production, which makes them a reasonable pick as long as the specific vehicle's service records show any relevant recalls or bulletins were addressed. On the current sixth generation platform, the 2022 and 2023 model years are generally considered more settled than the 2020 launch year, since Ford issued software updates and hardware revisions to address the early transmission hesitation complaints. The Explorer Hybrid, introduced for 2020, adds battery complexity but has not shown a distinct pattern of problems beyond the issues shared with the rest of the platform. As always, a well maintained, higher mileage Explorer with a full service history can be a smarter buy than a low mileage example with gaps in its paperwork, since consistent maintenance matters more to long term reliability than the model year alone.</p><h2>Ford Explorer Years to Avoid or Approach with Caution</h2><p>The 2011 through 2017 model years, and especially 2011 through 2015, are the ones worth researching most carefully. This is the generation tied to the exhaust odor and possible carbon monoxide complaints that prompted a National Highway Traffic Safety Administration investigation and led Ford to extend warranty coverage on certain exhaust related components and offer inspections or repairs on affected vehicles. If you are looking at an Explorer from this era, ask whether any related recall or service action work was completed, and have a mechanic check for exhaust smell in the cabin before you buy. These same years also carry the highest concentration of water pump and PTU complaints on 3.5L V6 and AWD models, so a cooling system inspection and a listen for whining noises from the front differential area are worthwhile on any pre-purchase check. On the newer end, the 2020 model year, the first of the redesigned rear drive platform, drew a notable number of complaints about hesitation and rough shifting shortly after launch. Ford addressed much of this through software updates, so a 2020 Explorer that has already had those updates applied is a lower risk purchase than one that has not.</p><h2>Is a Used Ford Explorer Worth Buying?</h2><p>None of this means the Explorer is a bad SUV to own. It offers a comfortable ride, a genuinely usable third row compared with many competitors, and strong towing capacity on V6 models, which makes it a practical family vehicle when it is running right. The problems above are common enough to show up repeatedly in owner forums and complaint databases, but they are not universal, and plenty of Explorers rack up well over 150,000 miles with routine maintenance. The key is going in with realistic expectations: budget for the possibility of a water pump or PTU repair on an older AWD V6 model, confirm exhaust related recall work was completed on any 2011-2017 vehicle, and have the SUV inspected by an independent mechanic before you buy. A clean pre-purchase inspection combined with documented service history goes a long way toward avoiding an expensive surprise down the road, and it can also give you leverage to negotiate the price if a repair is likely in the near future.</p><h2>How to Check a Specific Ford Explorer Before You Buy</h2><p>Generation wide problem patterns only tell part of the story. The individual vehicle you are looking at needs its own paper trail, especially since the Explorer is a popular choice for police departments, rental agencies, and corporate fleets, which can mean harder use than a typical private owner puts on a vehicle. Before you buy, run the vehicle's 17-character VIN and look for the following:</p><ul><li>Title brands such as salvage, rebuilt, flood, or lemon law buyback</li><li>Reported accidents or airbag deployments</li><li>Odometer readings that jump or do not match the vehicle's condition, a possible sign of rollback</li><li>Open, unresolved recalls, including exhaust and powertrain related service actions</li><li>Evidence of prior fleet, rental, or police use, which can mean a harder life even on a clean looking title</li></ul><p>A CarWorthIt VIN check pulls this information into one report, so you can see title status, salvage or auction damage records, odometer readings over time, open recalls, and prior fleet or rental use before you commit to a used Explorer. It is a quick way to confirm the SUV in front of you has not inherited one of the more expensive problems on this list, and it gives you real information to negotiate with if it has.</p>

faqs.q: What are the worst years for the Ford Explorer?
faqs.a: The 2011 through 2015 model years draw the most attention because they combine the exhaust odor and possible carbon monoxide complaints that led to an NHTSA investigation with the highest concentration of water pump and Power Transfer Unit failures. The first year 2020 redesign also had early transmission hesitation complaints, though many were addressed by software updates.

faqs.q: Is the Ford Explorer exhaust smell problem dangerous?
faqs.a: Any exhaust odor inside a cabin should be taken seriously, since it can indicate exhaust gases including carbon monoxide entering the vehicle. The issue on 2011-2017 Explorers prompted a federal investigation and led Ford to offer inspections and repairs on affected vehicles. If you smell exhaust in an Explorer, have it inspected before driving it further and confirm any related recall work has been completed.

faqs.q: Do all Ford Explorers have transmission problems?
faqs.a: No. Transmission hesitation complaints were concentrated in early production of the 2020 model year, the first year of the current rear wheel drive platform. Ford issued software updates to address much of the concern, and later model years such as 2022 and 2023 have not shown the same pattern of complaints.

faqs.q: How do I know if a used Explorer was a former police or fleet vehicle?
faqs.a: Look for clues like extra wiring, mounting holes on the dash or trunk, or a partition mount, but the most reliable method is running the VIN through a vehicle history check. A CarWorthIt report can flag prior fleet, rental, or police use along with title status and salvage or auction damage records so you know what you're actually buying.

title: Jeep Grand Cherokee Common Problems (and the Years to Avoid) in 2026
metaTitle: Jeep Grand Cherokee Common Problems (2026 Guide)
metaDescription: See the Jeep Grand Cherokee's most common problems, including TIPM failures and shifter recalls, plus which years to avoid and which to trust in 2026.
bodyHtml: <p>The Jeep Grand Cherokee has built a reputation as one of the more capable mid-size SUVs on the market, with real off-road ability, a comfortable ride, and engine choices ranging from an efficient V6 to a growling SRT V8. That reputation comes with a well-known asterisk, though. Grand Cherokees, especially those built in the early-to-mid 2010s, are also known for electrical gremlins, a notorious control module failure, and at least one widely covered recall involving the transmission shifter. None of that means you should rule out a used Grand Cherokee, but it does mean the model deserves a closer look than most before you hand over a down payment.</p><p>Below is a rundown of the problems that show up most often in owner forums, independent repair shops, and complaint databases, along with the years affected, the symptoms to watch for, and roughly what the repair tends to cost.</p><h2>Common Jeep Grand Cherokee Problems</h2><table><thead><tr><th>Problem</th><th>Affected Years / Generation</th><th>Symptoms</th><th>Rough Repair Cost</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>TIPM (Totally Integrated Power Module) failure</td><td>2011-2014 WK2, also seen on earlier Jeep models</td><td>No-start, fuel pump won't prime, random dash warning lights, wipers or interior lights turning on by themselves, dead battery</td><td>$800-$1,500</td></tr><tr><td>3.6L Pentastar V6 rocker arm and lifter tick</td><td>Early Pentastar engines, mostly 2011-2013</td><td>Ticking or knocking noise from the top of the engine, check engine light, in some cases camshaft or cylinder head damage if ignored</td><td>$1,500-$3,000+</td></tr><tr><td>Monostable gear-selector confusion</td><td>2014-2015 (facelift models with the rotary-style shifter)</td><td>Shifter doesn't return to a clear indicated position, driver may believe the vehicle is in Park when it is not; subject to a widely covered NHTSA recall</td><td>Free if the recall was completed; confirm it was done</td></tr><tr><td>Quadra-Lift air suspension failure</td><td>Higher-mileage examples across the WK2 generation (2011-2021)</td><td>Vehicle sitting low or uneven, compressor running constantly or not at all, ride height warning on the dash</td><td>$1,000-$2,500 per corner or compressor</td></tr><tr><td>Water leaks and interior electronics faults</td><td>Various years, especially sunroof-equipped models</td><td>Damp carpet, fogged interior lights, musty smell, intermittent electrical faults traced back to a wet module or connector</td><td>$200-$1,000 depending on the source and extent</td></tr></tbody></table><p>Not every Grand Cherokee from these years will develop these issues, and plenty of owners rack up well over 150,000 miles with nothing more than routine maintenance. But the complaint volume for these specific problems is high enough, and the repair costs steep enough, that they belong on your checklist before you buy.</p><h2>Most Reliable Grand Cherokee Years to Buy</h2><p>Jeep made real, documented improvements to the Grand Cherokee over the long run of the WK2 generation. The confusing rotary-style gear selector was replaced with a conventional shift lever starting with the 2016 model year, which removed that specific safety concern entirely. TIPM reliability also improved as the module and its wiring and weatherproofing were revised in later production years, and general owner complaints about random electrical faults thin out noticeably from around 2016 onward.</p><p>Because of that, model years roughly 2016 through 2021, the final year of the WK2 generation before the redesigned Grand Cherokee WL arrived for 2022, tend to draw fewer electrical complaints and are generally considered the sweet spot for a used buyer. Within that range, 2018 and newer trims paired with the updated 8-speed automatic and revised electronics are often singled out by owners as more trouble-free day to day. If your budget allows it, a well-maintained 2019 to 2021 Limited, Overland, or Trailhawk with a full service history and no outstanding recalls is typically a safer bet than an early-2010s example, even at similar mileage and price.</p><p>The newer WL generation, 2022 and later, is a different platform with its own early-production issues reported, including infotainment and software recalls, so it does not yet have the long track record that makes the mid-cycle WK2 years easy to recommend. If you are looking at a 2022 or newer model, treat it the way you would any new-to-market vehicle: check for open recalls and recent owner complaints rather than assuming the redesign solved everything.</p><h2>Grand Cherokee Years to Avoid or Approach Carefully</h2><p>The 2011 to 2013 model years combine two of the biggest headaches on this list. That is when the TIPM failures described above were most common, and it is also when the redesigned 3.6L Pentastar V6 was still in its first couple of production years, with a documented rocker arm and lifter tick that sometimes progressed into more serious top-end engine work if left unaddressed. Early Pentastars were not universally bad engines, but the complaint volume is high enough that a pre-purchase inspection is close to mandatory on any 2011 to 2013 Grand Cherokee.</p><p>The 2014 and 2015 model years add the monostable shifter problem on top of continuing electrical quirks carried over from the earlier design. Jeep and federal regulators addressed the shifter issue with a recall, and dealers reprogrammed the module and added an automatic park-and-shutdown safety feature, but not every 2014 or 2015 example on the road today has actually had that recall work completed. If you are considering one of these model years, confirming the recall history is not optional.</p><p>None of this means these years are unsalvageable purchases. Plenty are still on the road and running fine. But they are the years where skipping an inspection or a recall check is most likely to bite you later, and they are worth negotiating harder on if the seller cannot show the relevant service and recall records.</p><h2>Is a Used Grand Cherokee Worth Buying?</h2><p>For the right price and the right individual vehicle, yes. The Grand Cherokee offers a genuinely capable platform, a comfortable interior for its class, and a wide spread of trims and engines, so a well-sorted used example can be excellent value compared to a new one. The issues covered above are real and well documented, but they are also well known, which means independent mechanics who specialize in Jeep and Chrysler products have generally seen them before and know exactly what to check.</p><p>The practical approach is to treat every used Grand Cherokee as an individual vehicle rather than judging it purely by model year. Ask for maintenance records, have a pre-purchase inspection done by a shop that knows the platform, and pay particular attention to the battery and TIPM area for corrosion, the sound of the engine at cold start for any ticking, the air suspension's ride height and compressor operation if the trim has Quadra-Lift, and evidence of past water intrusion around the sunroof drains and carpet. A seller who balks at an independent inspection, or who cannot produce service history, is a bigger red flag than any single model year.</p><h2>How to Check a Specific Grand Cherokee Before You Buy</h2><p>Because the Grand Cherokee has been the subject of a genuinely high number of recalls over the years, covering everything from the shifter to airbags to software, checking the VIN of the specific vehicle you are considering matters more than it does for some other models. A VIN check will not replace a hands-on inspection, but it should confirm the following.</p><ul><li>Whether the title is clean or branded as salvage, rebuilt, or flood damaged</li><li>Whether the vehicle has a reported accident or airbag deployment on record</li><li>Whether the mileage history is consistent, to help rule out a rolled-back odometer</li><li>Whether there are open recalls, including shifter and TIPM-related campaigns, that have not yet been completed</li></ul><p>Running the VIN through CarWorthIt before you commit to a used Grand Cherokee gives you that background in minutes, so you can walk into the inspection knowing exactly what to ask the mechanic to focus on, rather than starting from scratch.</p>

faqs.q: What is the TIPM and why does it fail on Grand Cherokees?
faqs.a: The TIPM, or Totally Integrated Power Module, is a fuse and relay box mounted near the battery that controls a wide range of electrical functions, including the fuel pump. On 2011-2014 Grand Cherokees, and some earlier Jeep models, water intrusion and corrosion in that location have caused no-start conditions, fuel pump failures, and random electrical faults. Repairs typically run $800 to $1,500.

faqs.q: Are Jeep Grand Cherokees expensive to maintain?
faqs.a: Routine maintenance is comparable to other mid-size SUVs, but the Grand Cherokee has a few known repair categories, TIPM, early Pentastar engine work, and Quadra-Lift air suspension, that can be pricier than average when they occur. A well-maintained later model year with a clean history tends to be far cheaper to own than an early WK2 example with deferred repairs.

faqs.q: Which Grand Cherokee model year is the most reliable?
faqs.a: Owners and independent mechanics generally point to 2016 through 2021 as the strongest stretch of the WK2 generation, since the confusing shifter was redesigned starting in 2016 and TIPM-related electrical complaints dropped off noticeably from that point forward. Within that range, 2018 and newer trims are frequently cited as especially trouble-free.

faqs.q: How do I know if a used Grand Cherokee has open recalls?
faqs.a: The Grand Cherokee has been subject to a number of recalls over the years, including the well-known monostable shifter campaign on 2014-2015 models. Running the vehicle's VIN through a lookup tool, or checking directly with a Jeep dealer, will show any open recalls so you can confirm the work was completed before you buy.

title: Nissan Rogue Common Problems (and the Years to Avoid) in 2026
metaTitle: Nissan Rogue Common Problems & Years to Avoid
metaDescription: Thinking of buying a used Nissan Rogue? See which years have the worst CVT problems, what repairs cost, and how to check a VIN before you buy in 2026.
bodyHtml: <p>The Nissan Rogue is one of the best selling compact SUVs in America, prized for its comfortable ride, decent fuel economy, and low starting price on the used market. But that affordability comes with a catch: the Rogue relies on a continuously variable transmission (CVT) that has developed a rough reputation, especially in older model years. Before you buy a used Rogue in 2026, it pays to know which problems show up most often, which years to be cautious about, and how to protect yourself with a proper inspection and vehicle history check.</p><p>Below is a quick reference table of the issues most commonly reported by Rogue owners and independent mechanics, followed by a closer look at each generation.</p><table><thead><tr><th>Problem</th><th>Affected Years/Generation</th><th>Symptoms</th><th>Rough Repair Cost</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>CVT shuddering, hesitation, and failure</td><td>2014-2018 (second generation)</td><td>Jerking or shuddering under acceleration, delayed response from a stop, whining noise, warning lights, complete loss of power</td><td>$3,500-$5,500 for a CVT replacement; less for a rebuild</td></tr><tr><td>CVT overheating</td><td>2014-2018, especially higher-mileage examples</td><td>Transmission goes into limp mode, reduced power, overheating warning</td><td>$150-$400 for a cooler line or fluid service if caught early; much more if the unit is damaged</td></tr><tr><td>Fuel gauge and sending unit inaccuracy</td><td>2014-2020</td><td>Fuel gauge reads incorrectly, sudden drop to empty, inaccurate range estimate</td><td>$200-$450 for a sending unit replacement</td></tr><tr><td>EVAP and emissions system faults</td><td>2014-2020</td><td>Check engine light, small EVAP leak codes, failed emissions test</td><td>$150-$500 depending on the component (purge valve, canister, gas cap)</td></tr><tr><td>Brake wear and squeaking</td><td>All years, more common 2014-2019</td><td>Premature pad wear, grinding, squeaking at low speed</td><td>$150-$350 per axle for pads and rotors</td></tr><tr><td>Electrical gremlins (infotainment, sensors)</td><td>2014-2020</td><td>Backup camera glitches, Bluetooth dropouts, dash warning lights with no clear cause</td><td>$100-$600 depending on the fix</td></tr><tr><td>Interior wear (seats, trim, door panels)</td><td>2014-2019</td><td>Cracking dash plastic, worn seat bolsters, peeling trim</td><td>Cosmetic; $0-$300 for partial fixes</td></tr></tbody></table><h2>Which Rogue Years to Avoid</h2><p>If there is one issue that defines the used Rogue market, it is the CVT. Nissan has used continuously variable transmissions across most of its lineup since the mid 2000s, and the Rogue is no exception. The second generation Rogue, built from 2014 through 2018 (and closely related to the shorter-lived Rogue Select), has the worst reputation for CVT problems.</p><p>Owners of these model years have reported shuddering or jerking when accelerating from a stop, a delay before the vehicle actually moves, a whining or grinding noise from the transmission, and in the worst cases complete transmission failure well before 100,000 miles. Nissan extended the CVT warranty on many of these vehicles in response to owner complaints and lawsuits, but those extended warranties have expired on most used examples by now, which means a failed CVT is squarely the next owner's problem.</p><p>Within the 2014-2018 range, the 2014 and 2015 model years tend to draw the most complaints, though transmission issues have been reported throughout the generation. If you are shopping in this range, do not assume a vehicle is safe just because it is a later 2017 or 2018 model. Condition, maintenance history, and how the vehicle was driven matter as much as the model year.</p><p>Beyond the transmission, these same years are also where fuel gauge inaccuracy, EVAP faults, and general electrical quirks show up most often. None of these are usually deal breakers on their own, but combined with CVT risk they add up to a car that needs a careful look before you sign anything.</p><h2>Better and Newer Years to Consider</h2><p>Nissan redesigned the Rogue for the 2021 model year (third generation), and by most owner accounts the CVT in these newer vehicles has been more reliable, though it is still a CVT and still deserves respect. If your budget allows it, a 2021 or newer Rogue is generally a safer bet than the 2014-2018 generation.</p><p>If you need to stay in the second generation for budget reasons, a 2019 or 2020 model is typically a better choice than an earlier one. Nissan made incremental updates to the CVT software and cooling over the years, and later examples of that generation tend to have fewer reported transmission complaints, though they are not immune.</p><p>Whatever year you are considering, service history matters more than the badge on the back. A 2016 Rogue with documented, regular CVT fluid changes can be a safer buy than a 2019 with no service records at all.</p><h2>Is a Used Nissan Rogue Worth Buying?</h2><p>The Rogue can still be a smart used buy. It is affordable, roomy for its class, gets reasonable fuel economy, and parts and service are widely available since it is one of the most common SUVs on American roads. The key is proceeding with caution rather than avoiding the model altogether.</p><p>Before you buy any used Rogue, budget for and insist on a pre-purchase inspection that specifically checks the CVT. A qualified mechanic can look for fluid condition and color, listen for whining or shuddering during a test drive at varying speeds, check for any stored transmission fault codes with a scan tool, and confirm whether the CVT fluid has ever been serviced. Nissan recommends more frequent CVT fluid changes than many owners realize, and a Rogue that has never had its fluid touched is a bigger risk regardless of mileage.</p><p>Ask the seller directly whether the transmission has ever been serviced, repaired, or replaced. A replaced CVT under warranty is not necessarily a red flag; it can actually mean the weak part has already been swapped for a fresh unit. What you want to avoid is a car with unexplained shuddering, a seller who is vague about maintenance, or a suspiciously low price with no service records at all.</p><h2>How to Check a Specific Rogue Before You Buy</h2><p>A test drive and a mechanic's inspection tell you how the car feels today, but they will not tell you everything about its past. Before you hand over money for a used Nissan Rogue, it is worth running the vehicle identification number (VIN) through a history check to see the fuller picture.</p><p>A VIN check can help surface things a visual inspection cannot, including:</p><ul><li>Title brands such as salvage, rebuilt, or flood damage</li><li>Reported accidents or prior structural damage</li><li>Signs of an odometer rollback or inconsistent mileage readings</li><li>Open safety recalls, including any related to the CVT or fuel system, that have not been completed</li><li>Prior use as a rental, fleet, or lease vehicle</li></ul><p>Combining a clean VIN history with a mechanic's inspection focused on the CVT gives you the best chance of avoiding a used Rogue that turns into an expensive transmission repair a few months down the road. If you have a VIN in hand, run it through CarWorthIt before you commit, and use what you find to negotiate the price, request repairs, or walk away if something does not add up.</p>

faqs.q: What is the most common problem with the Nissan Rogue?
faqs.a: The most widely reported issue is the CVT (continuously variable transmission), particularly on 2014-2018 models. Owners describe shuddering or jerking during acceleration, hesitation from a stop, whining noises, and in some cases full transmission failure.

faqs.q: What years of Nissan Rogue should I avoid?
faqs.a: The 2014-2018 model years carry the most CVT-related complaints, with 2014 and 2015 drawing the highest number of reports. It is not a guarantee every one of these vehicles will fail, but they deserve extra scrutiny and a transmission-focused inspection before purchase.

faqs.q: How much does it cost to replace a Nissan Rogue CVT?
faqs.a: A full CVT replacement typically runs $3,500 to $5,500 depending on the shop and whether you use a new, remanufactured, or used unit. A CVT rebuild can sometimes cost less, but availability of qualified shops for CVT rebuilds varies by region.

faqs.q: Is the Nissan Rogue reliable overall?
faqs.a: Outside of the CVT, the Rogue is generally considered a reasonably dependable compact SUV with affordable parts and widespread service availability. The 2021-and-newer third generation has a better reported track record for transmission durability than the 2014-2018 generation.

title: Subaru Outback Common Problems (and the Best Years to Buy) in 2026
metaTitle: Subaru Outback Common Problems & Best Years (2026)
metaDescription: Thinking about buying a used Subaru Outback? Learn the common problems by generation, the best and worst years to buy, and how to check a VIN before you buy.
bodyHtml: <p>The Subaru Outback has spent decades building a reputation as the wagon that can do almost anything: haul the family, tackle a gravel forest road, handle a snowy mountain pass, and still return reasonable gas mileage along the way. Standard all-wheel drive, generous ground clearance, and a roomy interior have made it a favorite among outdoorsy families and daily commuters alike. But like any vehicle with a long production history, the Outback has some well-documented problem areas that show up more often in certain years than others.</p><p>If you are shopping for a used Outback in 2026, knowing which issues to expect, and which model years tend to be the safer bets, can save you from an expensive surprise a few months after you drive it home. Here is what actually tends to go wrong, and how to buy smart.</p><h2>Common Subaru Outback Problems</h2><p>Below are the issues that come up most often in owner forums, independent repair shop records, and Subaru's own service communications. Not every Outback will experience these, but they are the patterns worth checking for before you hand over a deposit.</p><table><thead><tr><th>Problem</th><th>Affected Years or Generation</th><th>Symptoms</th><th>Rough Repair Cost</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Head gasket leaks</td><td>Older 2.5L boxer engines, notably pre-2013 models</td><td>External oil or coolant seepage, coolant loss between top-offs, occasional overheating</td><td>$1,500 to $2,500</td></tr><tr><td>Excessive oil consumption</td><td>2011 to 2014, FB25 2.5L engine</td><td>Engine burns oil noticeably faster than normal between changes, low oil warning light appears early</td><td>Covered under a past extended warranty or class action settlement for eligible owners, otherwise $2,500 to $4,000 for piston ring work</td></tr><tr><td>CVT transmission concerns</td><td>Some 2015 and newer models</td><td>Shuddering at low speed, hesitation on acceleration, occasional harsh or jerky shifts</td><td>$3,500 to $6,000 for a replacement once out of warranty</td></tr><tr><td>Windshields crack easily</td><td>All years, frequently mentioned 2015 and up</td><td>Small rock chips spread into long cracks faster than on many other vehicles</td><td>$300 to $500 per replacement</td></tr><tr><td>Battery drain and electrical gremlins</td><td>Various years</td><td>Dead battery after sitting a few days, random dashboard warning lights, infotainment glitches</td><td>$150 to $400</td></tr><tr><td>Wheel bearing wear</td><td>All-wheel-drive models across most years</td><td>Humming or grinding noise from a wheel that gets louder as speed increases</td><td>$250 to $450 per bearing</td></tr></tbody></table><h2>Most Reliable Subaru Outback Years to Buy</h2><p>Subaru made real changes to the Outback's engines and transmissions over time, and the newer generations generally benefit from lessons learned on the older ones. Model years such as 2018 and 2019, and the redesigned sixth generation starting in 2020, tend to be popular picks with used car shoppers. By this point the FB25 engine's piston ring design had been revised to address the earlier oil consumption complaints, and Subaru had also rolled out software and hardware updates to smooth out CVT behavior on later production models.</p><p>Earlier in the Outback's history, well-maintained examples from around 2005 to 2009 can also be a reasonable choice, since these predate the FB25 oil consumption issue entirely. The tradeoff is that these older cars fall squarely into the window where head gasket leaks are more common, so a cooling system and gasket inspection matters even more on a car from this era.</p><p>In general, look for an Outback with full-service records, evidence of regular oil changes, and no signs of a check engine light being cleared right before the sale. A clean maintenance history tells you more than the model year alone.</p><h2>Subaru Outback Years to Be Cautious About</h2><p>The 2011 through 2014 Outback years, which use the FB25 2.5L engine, deserve the closest look. This engine developed a reputation for burning through oil faster than it should between scheduled changes, an issue significant enough that it became the subject of a class action settlement and an extended warranty program from Subaru covering piston ring replacement for eligible owners. If you are considering an Outback from these years, ask the seller directly about oil consumption history and check whether any warranty work was already performed.</p><p>Outbacks built before the FB engine arrived, roughly through the late 2000s and into the early 2010s, are the ones most associated with head gasket leaks on the older 2.5L boxer engine. A slow external oil or coolant leak is common and not always an emergency, but a neglected leak can lead to overheating and more serious engine damage, so it is worth having a mechanic check for weeping around the gasket seams and monitor coolant levels during a test drive.</p><p>Some early examples of the CVT-equipped Outbacks, particularly around the 2015 to 2016 model years, generated enough shuddering and hesitation complaints that Subaru extended CVT warranty coverage for some owners. A CVT replacement is one of the more expensive repairs on this vehicle, so a careful test drive that includes stop-and-go conditions and highway merging is worthwhile before buying a used example from this window.</p><h2>Is a Used Subaru Outback Worth Buying?</h2><p>For most buyers, yes. The Outback remains one of the more practical and capable options in its class, especially for anyone who regularly deals with rain, snow, or unpaved roads, since standard all-wheel drive is a genuine advantage over many two-wheel-drive crossovers. Fuel economy is reasonable for the size and utility offered, cargo space is generous, and later model years come with a strong suite of standard driver assistance features.</p><p>The key is matching your expectations to the specific model year and doing your homework before you buy rather than after. An Outback bought with awareness of these common issues, and with a pre-purchase inspection focused on the engine, transmission behavior, and cooling system, tends to be a dependable long-term vehicle. An Outback bought blind, without checking maintenance history, is where owners run into the expensive surprises described above.</p><h2>How to Check a Specific Outback Before You Buy</h2><p>Model year trends are useful for narrowing your search, but every individual Outback has its own history, and that history is what actually determines whether it is a good buy. Before you commit to a specific vehicle, it is worth pulling a VIN-based history report to check for things a test drive cannot always reveal, including:</p><ul><li>Salvage, rebuilt, or flood title brands from any state</li><li>Reported accidents or airbag deployments</li><li>Signs of odometer rollback or inconsistent mileage readings</li><li>Open manufacturer recalls, including any related to the CVT or oil consumption programs</li><li>Prior use as a rental, fleet, or lease vehicle</li></ul><p>A quick VIN check through CarWorthIt can surface these details in minutes, so you know exactly what you are dealing with before you negotiate a price or schedule a mechanic's inspection. Combined with a look at the specific problem areas for that model year, it is one of the simplest ways to buy a used Outback with confidence instead of guesswork.</p>

faqs.q: What is the most common problem with Subaru Outbacks?
faqs.a: Depending on the model year, the most frequently reported issues are head gasket leaks on older 2.5L engines built before the FB25 arrived, and excessive oil consumption on 2011 to 2014 models with the FB25 engine. Windshield cracking and occasional battery or electrical glitches are also commonly mentioned across nearly all model years.

faqs.q: Which Subaru Outback years should I avoid?
faqs.a: No Outback year should be ruled out automatically, but 2011 to 2014 models deserve extra scrutiny for oil consumption history, older pre-FB25 models should be checked for head gasket leaks, and early CVT-equipped models around 2015 to 2016 should get a thorough test drive to rule out shuddering or hesitation.

faqs.q: Are Subaru Outbacks expensive to maintain?
faqs.a: Routine maintenance costs are generally in line with other mid-size crossovers and wagons. Where costs can climb is if you end up with a vehicle that needs a head gasket repair, CVT replacement, or piston ring work, which is why checking a vehicle's specific history and maintenance records before buying matters so much.

faqs.q: Is the Subaru Outback reliable for long-distance or winter driving?
faqs.a: Yes, this is one of the Outback's strongest selling points. Standard all-wheel drive, solid ground clearance, and a comfortable ride make it a popular choice for winter climates and road trips, provided you choose a well-maintained example and stay on top of the issues common to its specific model year.

title: Toyota Tacoma Common Problems (and the Best Years to Buy) in 2026
metaTitle: Toyota Tacoma Common Problems & Best Years 2026
metaDescription: Discover common Toyota Tacoma problems by model year, including transmission and frame rust issues, plus the most reliable Tacoma years to buy used in 2026.
bodyHtml: <p>The Toyota Tacoma has built a reputation as one of the most dependable midsize pickups sold in the United States, and used examples routinely command some of the strongest resale values of any vehicle on the road. That reputation is earned, but it does not mean every Tacoma on a used car lot is problem free. Across three generations spanning 1995 through today, certain model years have developed well-documented weak points, from transmission behavior in early third-gen trucks to frame corrosion on older second-gen models. If you are shopping for a used Tacoma in 2026, knowing which years carry which issues, and which years are considered the sweet spot, can save you thousands of dollars and give you real negotiating leverage. Here is what actually shows up in owner reports, technical service bulletins, and independent shop experience.</p><h2>Toyota Tacoma Common Problems: Quick Reference Table</h2><table><thead><tr><th>Problem</th><th>Affected Years / Generation</th><th>Symptoms</th><th>Rough Repair Cost</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>6-speed automatic transmission hunting/harsh shifts</td><td>2016-2017 (early third gen)</td><td>Gear hunting on light throttle, delayed downshifts, harsh engagement</td><td>$0 (reflash) to $1,500-$3,000 (valve body/repair)</td></tr><tr><td>Frame rust/corrosion</td><td>2005-2010 (second gen), worse in salt-belt states</td><td>Visible rust on frame rails, rear crossmember, leaf spring hangers</td><td>$5,000+ for frame replacement, can total the truck</td></tr><tr><td>Rear leaf spring clunk/rough ride</td><td>All generations, more reports in cold climates</td><td>Clunking or creaking over bumps, stiff unloaded ride</td><td>$200-$600 for bushings/shackles</td></tr><tr><td>Lower ball joint wear</td><td>2005-2008 (early second gen)</td><td>Clunk from front end, uneven tire wear, steering looseness</td><td>$300-$700 per side</td></tr><tr><td>Oil consumption</td><td>Some 2005-2010 4.0L V6 engines</td><td>Oil level drops between changes, needs regular top-ups</td><td>Varies; low oil risk of engine damage if ignored</td></tr></tbody></table><h2>2016-2017 Automatic Transmission Hunting and Harsh Shifts</h2><p>When the third-generation Tacoma launched for 2016, the new 6-speed automatic transmission drew a wave of owner complaints. Drivers reported the transmission hunting between fifth and sixth gear on light highway throttle, along with hesitation off the line and occasionally harsh downshifts when slowing for traffic. Toyota issued multiple technical service bulletins addressing the behavior, and dealers were able to resolve many cases with an ECU reflash under warranty. A smaller number of trucks needed valve body work or a transmission rebuild once the factory warranty had expired. If you are looking at a 2016 or 2017 Tacoma, drive it on both surface streets and the highway, pay attention to how it behaves at partial throttle on a grade, and ask whether the transmission software has already been updated.</p><h2>Frame Rust on 2005-2010 Tacomas</h2><p>Frame corrosion is the most serious problem associated with older Tacomas, particularly second-generation trucks built from 2005 through 2010 that spent time in states that use road salt in the winter. Toyota faced significant consumer complaints and litigation over premature frame rust on trucks of this era, and the company extended corrosion warranties and, in some cases, replaced frames on affected vehicles registered in eligible states. Perforation typically shows up first around the rear leaf spring hangers, the rear crossmember, and the frame rails behind the cab. Even if a truck is currently registered somewhere warm and dry, check its title and registration history, because a Tacoma that spent its first several years in the Rust Belt or Northeast can carry hidden frame damage no matter where it is sold today. Have any second-gen Tacoma inspected on a lift before you buy.</p><h2>Rear Leaf Spring Clunk and Rough Ride</h2><p>The Tacoma uses a leaf spring rear suspension across every generation, which gives it strong towing and payload capability but also a noticeably stiffer, choppier ride when the bed is empty. That firmness is by design and is not itself a defect, but many owners also report a clunking or creaking noise from worn leaf spring bushings and shackles, especially in cold weather or over rough pavement. Toyota has released service bulletins covering bushing lubrication and replacement for this complaint. It is an inexpensive fix in isolation, but it is worth budgeting for on any higher-mileage truck and worth listening for during a test drive over speed bumps or uneven road surfaces.</p><h2>Lower Ball Joint Wear on 2005-2008 Trucks</h2><p>Early second-generation Tacomas, roughly 2005 through 2008, are known for premature wear in the front lower ball joints. Symptoms include a clunking noise from the front suspension, uneven or accelerated tire wear, and looseness in the steering feel at speed. Toyota addressed the issue with a service campaign covering some trucks within that window, though not every affected vehicle may have had the work performed. Have a mechanic put the front end on a lift and check for play in the ball joints as part of any pre-purchase inspection on a Tacoma from this era.</p><h2>Oil Consumption on Some Second-Gen V6 Engines</h2><p>Toyota's V6 and V8 engines from the mid-2000s have a documented history of above-average oil consumption in some applications, and a portion of second-generation Tacoma owners with the 4.0L V6 report needing to top off oil between changes more often than expected. It is generally less widespread than the oil consumption issues seen on Toyota's larger V8 trucks and SUVs of the same era, but it shows up often enough in owner forums and repair records to be worth checking. Ask the seller for maintenance records showing oil change intervals and any top-ups, and check the dipstick yourself before you buy, since running an engine low on oil for extended periods can lead to expensive internal damage.</p><h2>Most Reliable Toyota Tacoma Years to Buy</h2><p>Once you get past the launch-year quirks of each generation, the Tacoma's overall reliability record is strong. Within the third generation, 2018 through 2020 models are widely considered a sweet spot, since Toyota had already addressed the early transmission software complaints from 2016 and 2017 through updates and running changes, while still offering the older, simpler infotainment system that many owners find easier to live with than the newer touchscreen units. Model years 2021 through 2023 carry that same drivetrain reliability with updated infotainment and safety tech, and remain a solid choice if you want a newer truck with fewer known issues. Within the second generation, 2011 through 2015 trucks tend to have fewer complaints than the 2005-2008 range, since Toyota had resolved the early ball joint and worked through some of the frame concerns by then, though frame inspection is still worthwhile on any older truck.</p><h2>Tacoma Years to Be Cautious About</h2><p>As with most redesigned vehicles, the first model year of a generation tends to carry the most reported issues. That makes 2016 worth extra scrutiny for the transmission behavior described above, and it makes 2005 worth extra scrutiny for both the ball joint wear and the earliest frame corrosion complaints, since running changes and revised parts tend to arrive a model year or two into a redesign. That does not mean every 2005 or 2016 Tacoma has a problem. It means those trucks deserve a closer pre-purchase inspection and a look at whether previous owners already had the relevant service bulletin work performed.</p><h2>Is a Used Toyota Tacoma Worth Buying in 2026?</h2><p>For most buyers, yes. The Tacoma consistently holds its value better than almost any other midsize truck on the used market, which reflects genuine demand for its off-road capability, simple mechanicals, and long-term durability. Parts availability is excellent, independent shops are comfortable working on them, and a well-maintained Tacoma can reasonably be expected to cover well over 200,000 miles. The tradeoff is that strong demand also means used Tacoma prices stay high relative to the vehicle's age, so you are paying a premium for that reputation. It also means you need to look past the badge and check the individual truck in front of you, since Tacomas are popular work trucks and off-road vehicles, and a rough life off pavement or in commercial use will not always be obvious just from a test drive.</p><h2>How to Check a Specific Tacoma Before You Buy</h2><p>Knowing the common problem areas by model year is a good starting point, but it will not tell you the condition of the exact truck you are about to buy. Before you hand over payment, run the VIN and confirm the following:</p><ul><li>Title brand, including salvage, rebuilt, or flood titles that may not show up on a casual look at the paperwork</li><li>Reported accidents or structural damage, which matters even more on a truck that may have seen off-road use</li><li>Odometer consistency across past inspections and title transfers, to rule out rollback</li><li>Open safety recalls tied to that specific VIN that have not yet been completed</li><li>Prior use as a fleet, rental, or commercial work vehicle, which can mean harder wear than the mileage alone suggests</li></ul><p>A CarWorthIt VIN check pulls this history together in one report, so you can see title status, salvage or auction damage records, odometer readings over time, and open recalls before you commit to a truck. Running the VIN takes a couple of minutes and can save you from buying a Tacoma with problems that are not visible in a driveway walk-around or a short test drive.</p>

faqs.q: What is the most common problem with Toyota Tacomas?
faqs.a: It depends on the generation. Early third-gen trucks (2016-2017) are best known for automatic transmission hunting and harsh shifts, while older second-gen trucks (2005-2010) are more associated with frame rust and, in the earliest years, front lower ball joint wear. There is no single defect that affects every Tacoma; the issues are tied to specific model years.

faqs.q: Which Toyota Tacoma model years should I avoid?
faqs.a: No Tacoma year should be ruled out automatically, but 2005 and 2016 deserve extra caution and a thorough pre-purchase inspection, since they were the first model years of their respective generations and carry the highest concentration of owner-reported issues, including ball joint wear and transmission hunting.

faqs.q: Do all used Toyota Tacomas have frame rust?
faqs.a: No. Frame rust is mainly a concern on 2005-2010 Tacomas that spent significant time in states that use road salt in winter, such as the Northeast and Midwest. Tacomas that have always lived in warm, dry climates are far less likely to show significant corrosion, but any older truck should still be inspected on a lift before purchase.

faqs.q: How can I check if a specific Tacoma has hidden problems before buying?
faqs.a: Run the vehicle's VIN through a service like CarWorthIt to check for title brands, salvage or auction damage records, odometer inconsistencies, open safety recalls, and prior use as a fleet or work vehicle. Combine that report with a mechanic's pre-purchase inspection and a test drive that covers both highway and stop-and-go conditions.

title: Honda Civic Common Problems and the Best Years to Buy in 2026
metaTitle: Honda Civic Common Problems & Best Years to Buy
metaDescription: Honda Civic common problems by model year, including 1.5L turbo oil dilution and AC condenser failures, plus the most reliable Civic years to buy used in 2026.
bodyHtml: <p>The Honda Civic has been one of the best selling compact cars in the United States for decades, and used Civics tend to hold their value better than almost anything else in the class. That reputation is well earned, but it does not mean every Civic on a used car lot is trouble free. Certain model years and engines carry documented problems, ranging from a turbocharged engine that can dilute its own oil with gasoline to air conditioning systems that fail years before they should. Knowing which issues go with which generation, and which years Honda got right, can save you real money before you ever sign a bill of sale.</p><h2>Common Honda Civic Problems at a Glance</h2><p>Before digging into the details, here is a quick reference table of the most frequently reported Civic problems, the years and generations most affected, the symptoms to watch for, and roughly what repairs tend to cost out of pocket.</p><table><thead><tr><th>Problem</th><th>Affected Years / Generation</th><th>Symptoms</th><th>Rough Repair Cost</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Turbo engine fuel dilution</td><td>2016-2018 (1.5L turbo)</td><td>Rising oil level, gasoline smell on the dipstick, possible knocking or a check engine light</td><td>$0 under extended warranty; $3,000-$6,000 for engine repair if not covered</td></tr><tr><td>AC condenser failure</td><td>2016-2018 (10th gen)</td><td>Warm air from the vents, visible refrigerant leaks near the front of the car</td><td>$600-$1,200 per repair</td></tr><tr><td>Infotainment / Bluetooth glitches</td><td>2016-2019 (10th gen)</td><td>Screen freezing or rebooting, dropped phone pairing, laggy backup camera</td><td>$0 for a software update; $400-$800 for head unit replacement</td></tr><tr><td>Engine block cracking</td><td>2006-2011 (1.8L, 8th gen)</td><td>Slow coolant loss, overheating, white exhaust smoke</td><td>$3,000-$5,500 for engine replacement</td></tr><tr><td>Paint / clear coat peeling</td><td>Most common on 2001-2005, some 2006-2011</td><td>Clear coat flaking or peeling, especially on the roof and hood</td><td>$500-$2,500 for a partial repaint</td></tr><tr><td>CVT shudder / hesitation</td><td>2014-2015; some 2022 and newer</td><td>Hesitation pulling away from a stop, low speed shudder, whining under load</td><td>$0 for software recalibration; $3,000-$4,000 for CVT replacement</td></tr></tbody></table><h2>1.5L Turbo Engine Fuel Dilution (2016-2018)</h2><p>The tenth generation Civic introduced a turbocharged 1.5L four cylinder that quickly became known for a specific and well documented flaw: unburned gasoline seeping past the piston rings and mixing with the engine oil, especially during cold starts and short trips in colder climates. Owners reported the oil level on the dipstick rising over time and smelling like fuel, which thins the oil and reduces its ability to properly lubricate the engine. Honda acknowledged the issue, released updated engine control software aimed at reducing it, and extended the powertrain warranty for a number of affected 2016 through 2018 Civics (and the related CR-V) in many cases. If you are looking at one of these model years, ask for service records confirming the software update was performed, and check the oil level and smell it during any pre-purchase inspection.</p><h2>AC Condenser Failures (2016-2018)</h2><p>Another widely reported complaint on the 10th generation Civic is premature air conditioning condenser failure. The condenser sits low and forward in the engine bay, which leaves it more exposed to road debris, salt, and stone chips than on some competitors. Owners have reported the AC blowing warm air within just a few years of ownership, often traced back to a cracked or leaking condenser rather than normal wear and tear. Replacement typically requires a new condenser, a system evacuation and recharge, and sometimes a new receiver drier, which adds up quickly in parts and labor. If you are test driving a 2016 to 2018 Civic in warm weather, run the AC on max for several minutes and confirm it blows consistently cold air the whole time.</p><h2>Infotainment and Bluetooth Glitches</h2><p>The touchscreen infotainment system introduced alongside the 10th generation Civic drew criticism almost immediately for freezing, slow response times, dropped Bluetooth connections, and a backup camera image that lagged or occasionally failed to display. Much of this was addressed through dealer software updates over the years, and Honda revised the system's hardware and layout for later model years, but it is still worth testing thoroughly on any used example. Pair your phone, confirm the backup camera comes up instantly in reverse, and cycle through the screen menus looking for lag or freezing before you buy.</p><h2>Engine Block Cracking on the 1.8L (2006-2011)</h2><p>Going further back, the 8th generation Civic's 1.8L engine developed a reputation among some owners and independent mechanics for engine block cracking, typically showing up as slow coolant loss, overheating, or white exhaust smoke with no obvious external leak. It was never a defect affecting every 1.8L Civic on the road, but it came up often enough in owner forums and repair shop reports that it is worth flagging for anyone shopping a 2006 through 2011 Civic. A cooling system pressure test and a look at the coolant reservoir for oil contamination (or the oil for coolant contamination) during an inspection can help rule this out before you buy.</p><h2>Paint Peeling and Occasional Windshield Complaints on Older Civics</h2><p>Clear coat peeling, particularly on the roof and hood, shows up on a number of early and mid 2000s Civics that spent years parked outside in strong sun. It is a cosmetic issue rather than a mechanical one, but it can be expensive to fix properly, since a partial repaint is usually the only real solution once the clear coat starts flaking. Some owners of older Civics have also reported windshields that seem to chip or crack more easily than expected. This has not been confirmed as a manufacturing defect and is more likely a mix of thinner factory glass and ordinary bad luck with road debris, but it is worth a close look at the glass before buying an older car.</p><h2>CVT Shudder and Hesitation</h2><p>Civics equipped with a continuously variable transmission, which became standard on most trims starting with the 2014 model year, have drawn occasional complaints of shuddering at low speeds, hesitation when accelerating from a stop, or a whining noise under load. This has not reached the scale of the CVT problems reported on some other brands, but a handful of model years, including some early CVT-equipped Civics and a portion of the redesigned 2022 and newer cars, have had complaints serious enough that a software recalibration, or in rarer cases a transmission replacement, was needed. Test drive any CVT-equipped Civic through a full range of speeds, including stop and go traffic, and pay attention to hesitation or shudder before you accelerate hard.</p><h2>The Most Reliable Honda Civic Years to Buy</h2><ul><li><strong>2013 Civic:</strong> the 9th generation received a mid cycle refresh that fixed much of the cheap interior and soft handling criticism from the 2012 launch year, and it predates the 1.5L turbo issues entirely.</li><li><strong>2019-2021 Civic:</strong> still the 10th generation, but built after Honda updated the engine control software and made running changes to the AC condenser and infotainment system, making these later years noticeably more trouble free than 2016-2018.</li><li><strong>2022 and newer Civic:</strong> the redesigned 11th generation has earned strong reliability marks and better build quality, and is a solid choice once it is available at used-car prices.</li></ul><h2>Honda Civic Years to Be Cautious About</h2><ul><li><strong>2006-2011 (8th gen, 1.8L):</strong> engine block cracking reports, plus the general rust and wear that come with an older car.</li><li><strong>2012 Civic:</strong> the 9th generation's launch year drew criticism for cheap interior materials and softer handling; not unreliable, just the weakest year of that generation before the 2013 refresh.</li><li><strong>2016-2018 Civic:</strong> the highest concentration of turbo fuel dilution and AC condenser complaints. Not every one of these cars will have a problem, but they deserve the closest inspection.</li></ul><h2>Is a Used Honda Civic Worth Buying?</h2><p>Yes, for most buyers a used Civic remains one of the smarter choices in the compact segment. It holds its resale value well, parts and independent mechanics are easy to find anywhere in the country, and the issues covered in this guide are well documented rather than mysterious, which makes them easy to check for before you buy. The key is matching the model year to your budget and being willing to walk away from a specific car, not an entire generation, if the inspection turns up red flags. A 2017 Civic with full service records and a documented software update is a very different purchase than a 2017 Civic with no history at all.</p><h2>How to Check a Specific Civic Before You Buy</h2><p>Every issue in this guide can show up, or not show up, on any individual car depending on how it was maintained, where it was driven, and plain luck.
